data.title: Yubico (OM:YUBICO) Net Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Growth Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Yubico (OM:YUBICO) FY 2025 earnings snapshot

Yubico (OM:YUBICO) has wrapped up FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of 548.3 million SEK and basic EPS of 0.04 SEK, setting the tone for a year where trailing 12 month revenue came in at 2.2 billion SEK and basic EPS was 1.49 SEK. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between 499.1 million SEK and 623.1 million SEK over the past year, with basic EPS ranging from 0.04 SEK to 0.74 SEK. Trailing 12 month EPS and net income have tracked alongside 12 month revenue in the low single digit billions of SEK. For investors, the key question now is how the current net margin compression shapes the quality and durability of those earnings.

See our full analysis for Yubico.

With the scorecard set, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing stories about Yubico, where growth expectations and margin pressures pull in different directions.

See what the community is saying about Yubico

Net margin slips to 5.8%

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Yubico earned net income of SEK128.1 million on SEK2.2b of revenue, which works out to a 5.8% net margin compared with 16% a year earlier.
  • Bears focus on that margin compression as a sign that hardware focused economics are under pressure, yet
    • they also assume in their own narrative that margins could reach 13.7% in about three years, which would be higher than the current 5.8% even though recent quarters include Q4 FY 2025 net income of just SEK3.7 million on SEK548.3 million of revenue, a relatively thin quarter.
    • this gap between today’s 5.8% trailing margin and the implied 13.7% margin view shows that even cautious analysts are anchoring their concerns to what happens from here, not to the latest reported profitability alone.
If you are weighing whether this margin drop is a temporary blip or a longer term issue, it helps to see how skeptics frame the downside case in detail: 🐻 Yubico Bear Case

EPS swings against bullish hopes

  • Quarterly basic EPS for FY 2025 moved from SEK0.59 in Q1 to SEK0.74 in Q3, then down to SEK0.04 in Q4, while trailing 12 month EPS fell from SEK4.32 in Q4 FY 2024 to SEK1.49 by Q4 FY 2025.
  • Bulls talk about earnings growing roughly 38.4% per year over the next few years and, in their scenario, profits rising from SEK254.5 million today to SEK719.2 million by around 2028, yet
    • the current trailing EPS of SEK1.49 and Q4 EPS of SEK0.04 show that, for now, earnings sit a long way below those bullish outcomes and have moved down from the SEK4.32 trailing EPS level seen a year earlier.
    • this contrast means anyone leaning on the bullish view is effectively saying the subscription shift and enterprise roll outs will need to lift earnings from today’s reset base, not from the higher profitability implied by last year’s trailing numbers.
If you want to see how optimistic investors connect these EPS swings to their long term story, the full bullish case lays it out: 🐂 Yubico Bull Case

High P/E and big DCF gap

  • At a share price of SEK54.08, Yubico trades on a trailing P/E of 36.5x compared with a Swedish software industry average of 20.4x, while also sitting well below the stated DCF fair value of SEK134.33 and an allowed analyst price target of SEK85.00.
  • Consensus style views point to forecast revenue growth of about 12.7% per year and earnings growth around 38.4% per year, and that mix creates a tension where
    • the elevated 36.5x P/E versus the 20.4x industry level reflects investors paying more for each krona of trailing earnings, even though trailing margins have moved from 16% to 5.8%.
    • at the same time, the gap between SEK54.08 and SEK134.33 on the DCF fair value, as well as the SEK85.00 analyst target, shows how much of the upside case depends on those growth forecasts materialising over the next few years.

data.title: Telus (TSX:T) Margin Improvement Tests Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
TELUS (TSX:T) has put fresh numbers on the table for FY 2025, with third quarter revenue at CA$5.1b and basic EPS of CA$0.32 alongside net income of CA$493m as investors weigh the latest move in margins. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between CA$4.9b and CA$5.3b over the past six reported periods, while basic EPS has ranged from roughly CA$0.00 to CA$0.32. This sets a clear backdrop for how current earnings fit within its recent history. With net profit margins now sitting above last year, the focus for shareholders is increasingly on how sustainable that profitability profile looks from here.

See our full analysis for TELUS.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to line these results up against the dominant narratives around TELUS’s growth, risk and income profile to see which stories hold up and which start to look stretched.

See what the community is saying about TELUS

Advertisement

Net margin at 5.8% puts more weight on profitability quality

  • On a trailing basis TELUS reports a 5.8% net profit margin versus 4.6% a year earlier, and CA$1.2b of net income on CA$20.4b of revenue, so a bigger share of each dollar of sales is currently turning into profit.
  • What stands out against the bullish view is that, while bulls are looking for margins to reach 8.7%, the last twelve months are still anchored around 5.8%,
    • consensus of modest earnings growth of about 5.5% a year from this base supports the idea of steady progress but does not yet point to the step change in profitability that the most optimistic margin targets would imply,
    • and the earlier CA$591m one off loss in the trailing period means bulls need to separate that one time impact from the underlying margin trend when they judge how realistic those higher targets are.
On top of this set of numbers, some bulls argue the current profitability is just a starting point and see room for more improvement as TELUS leans into higher margin businesses like health and AI enabled services, as well as cost savings from digitisation and capital light partnerships. 🐂 TELUS Bull Case

P/E of 24.9x and 8.83% yield put pressure on cash coverage

  • The shares trade on a 24.9x P/E compared with 16.5x for the Global Telecom group and 9.2x for peers, while at the same time carrying an 8.83% dividend yield that is flagged as not being covered by either earnings or free cash flow.
  • Critics highlight that this combination challenges the bearish claim that the share price already reflects all the financial strain,
    • because weak interest coverage and a dividend that is not supported by current cash flows mean a lot of today’s return is coming from a payout that is stretching the balance sheet,
    • and with the share price at CA$18.96, any investor weighing the 8.83% income stream against those coverage issues has to think carefully about how much risk they are taking on for that level of yield.
Skeptics warn that if interest coverage and cash generation do not improve, the mix of a premium P/E, high dividend yield and elevated leverage could keep pressure on how the market values TELUS over time. 🐻 TELUS Bear Case

DCF fair value of CA$51.07 contrasts with 3.6% revenue growth forecast

  • The DCF fair value used in the analysis is CA$51.07 per share versus the current price of CA$18.96, while analysts are looking for revenue to grow about 3.6% a year and earnings about 5.5% a year from a trailing base of CA$20.4b of revenue and CA$1.2b of net income.
  • Consensus narrative notes that this large gap between the DCF fair value and the share price sits alongside only mid single digit forecast growth,
    • with earnings up 27.7% over the last year but down about 10% a year over five years, so the recent improvement still sits against a weaker multi year trend,
    • and that mix of modest growth forecasts, a premium 24.9x P/E and the CA$51.07 DCF fair value means investors need to decide whether the current price is simply cautious or whether it reflects ongoing concerns about leverage and the sustainability of the 8.83% dividend.

This kind of regulated buyback activity can influence metrics such as earnings per share over time. It can also offer investors a clearer view of how management thinks about Viscofan’s financial position and long term value creation.

For a profitable food sector company like Viscofan, this is a commonly used yardstick because earnings are a core driver of long term returns.

That mix of a modest discount to sector averages and a small premium to fair ratio gives investors a tight reference band for judging whether the current price feels comfortable.

data.title: Entra (OB:ENTRA) One Off Gain Lifts Profitability And Complicates Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Entra (OB:ENTRA) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of NOK 780 million and basic EPS of NOK 1.89, rounding out a year in which quarterly revenue stayed in a tight NOK 762 million to NOK 780 million band and EPS ranged from NOK 1.02 to NOK 2.18. The company has seen revenue move from NOK 756 million in Q4 2024 to NOK 780 million in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from NOK 3.18 in Q4 2024 to NOK 1.89 in Q4 2025. This sets up a year where investors are likely to focus on how underlying margins are holding up across the portfolio.

See our full analysis for Entra.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the dominant narratives around Entra, and where the figures start to challenge those storylines.

See what the community is saying about Entra

Advertisement

TTM profitability helped by NOK 305m one off

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Entra earned NOK 1.2b of net income excluding extra items on NOK 3.1b of revenue, and that figure includes a NOK 305m one off gain that lifted the result.
  • Bulls highlight that Entra has moved from losses a year ago to profit, which lines up with the data, but the one off gain means:
    • TTM EPS of NOK 6.33 compares with very weak EPS figures in 2024 (including a period with EPS of NOK 19.36 loss). The swing into profit is therefore partly mechanical rather than purely from recurring operations.
    • Forecast earnings growth of about 6.4% a year is being judged on this uplifted base. Investors need to separate the NOK 305m boost from the underlying NOK 1.2b of net income when thinking about how durable that growth might be.
Entra’s recent move back into profit is exactly what optimists have been waiting for, but bulls themselves acknowledge that a NOK 305m boost makes it harder to tell how much of the turnaround is repeatable, which is what really matters over the next few years. 🐂 Entra Bull Case

Interest coverage flagged as major weak spot

  • The risk summary calls out that earnings in the last 12 months do not comfortably cover interest expenses, even with NOK 1.2b of net income and NOK 3.1b of revenue on a trailing basis.
  • Bears focus on this weak interest coverage as a core concern, and the recent numbers give them some support:
    • Five year earnings have fallen at about 44.5% a year, so the current profit level sits against a backdrop of long term earnings pressure that can make servicing debt more sensitive to any setback.
    • With forecasts pointing to only about 2.5% yearly revenue growth, any rise in funding costs or slower rental growth would leave less room for interest to be paid comfortably out of ongoing income.
Skeptics point out that a capital intensive property owner with weak interest coverage is exposed if funding stays demanding, and the combination of modest forecast revenue growth and a long term earnings decline trend gives that argument real bite. 🐻 Entra Bear Case

P/E around 18x against DCF fair value of NOK 4.19

  • Entra trades on a trailing P/E of about 18x at a share price of NOK 113.60, close to peer levels, while a DCF fair value in the data sits far lower at NOK 4.19.
  • Consensus narrative leans on recurring cash flows from Norwegian offices, and the tension between these valuation markers is clear:
    • The 18x P/E is in line with direct peers but higher than the 14x average for the wider European real estate sector, suggesting the market is willing to pay a premium for Entra’s current earnings stream.
    • The DCF fair value of NOK 4.19, far below both the NOK 113.60 share price and an analyst target of NOK 114.00, reflects a much more cautious view of long term cash flows than the multiple based pricing implies.

data.title: Founder Returns As GEO Group Activates US$520m Contracts And Seeks Value

GEO Group operates correctional, detention, and community reentry facilities, so leadership changes can matter for how contracts are pursued, managed, and renewed. The announcement comes alongside significant operational growth, with multiple new or expanded agreements and several large facilities being activated. These developments can shape how investors view NYSE:GEO as a going concern. data.title: Is Heineken (ENXTAM:HEIA) Pricing Look Interesting After Recent Share Price Strength
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Heineken at around €78 a share still offers value, the key question is how its current price compares with what the business might reasonably be worth.
  • The stock has recently been firm, with returns of 6.0% over the last 7 days, 14.4% over the last 30 days, 13.2% year to date, and 0.8% over the last year. However, the 3 year and 5 year returns of negative 12.9% and negative 1.0% show a much flatter longer term picture.
  • Recent news around Heineken has focused on its position as a major global brewer and how it is managing brand strength and operations in different markets. This helps frame how investors think about its long term prospects. These themes often influence how the market prices the shares, particularly when sentiment shifts between focusing on shorter term share price moves and longer term fundamentals.
  • Right now, Heineken scores 3 out of 6 on our valuation checks, giving it a valuation score of 3. Next we will look at what different valuation methods indicate about that number, before finishing with a way of thinking about value that can give you a fuller picture than any single model.

Find out why Heineken's 0.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Heineken Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all those future euros are worth in today’s money.

For Heineken, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about €2.57b. Analysts have provided forecasts out to 2028, with Simply Wall St then extrapolating free cash flow out to 2035. For example, projected free cash flow for 2026 is €2.66b and for 2035 is €3.39b, with intermediate years gradually stepping up between those figures.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €150.96 per share. Compared with a recent share price around €78, this output suggests the stock is 48.3% undervalued according to this DCF model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Heineken is undervalued by 48.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 234 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Heineken.

Approach 2: Heineken Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Heineken, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how risky they feel those earnings are.

Heineken currently trades on a P/E of 23.05x. That sits above the Beverage industry average of 17.72x, but slightly below the peer group average of 24.82x. On their own, these comparisons show where the market is pricing Heineken relative to its sector and closest competitors, but they do not fully reflect the company’s specific growth outlook, profitability or risk profile.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio tackles that by estimating what a company’s P/E might be given factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and identified risks. For Heineken, this Fair Ratio is 21.36x, which is below the current 23.05x P/E. On this basis, the shares screens as somewhat expensive relative to what the Fair Ratio model suggests would be a more neutral level.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 100 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Heineken Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, which are simply the stories investors tell about a company that link its business outlook to a set of numbers like future revenue, earnings, margins and a fair value. On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives to set your own view on Heineken, compare it with others, and see in real time how each view changes when new earnings or news arrive. For example, one Heineken Narrative might lean closer to a fair value around €64.62 with more cautious assumptions, while another leans toward €110.19 with more optimistic expectations. By comparing each Narrative’s Fair Value with today’s share price, you get a clear, easy to use framework for assessing whether the stock looks expensive, cheap or roughly in line with your own story.

For Heineken however we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Heineken Narratives:

🐂 Heineken Bull Case

Fair value in this bull case: €85.16 per share

Implied discount to this fair value at a €78.08 share price: about 8.3% below the narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption used in this view: 3.73% a year

  • Focuses on premium brands, emerging markets and low or no alcohol products as key drivers of revenue and margin strength.
  • Assumes gradual improvement in profit margins and steady earnings growth, supported by cost savings and digital efficiency programs.
  • Flags currency swings, reliance on emerging markets and pressure in mature European markets as the main things that could upset this story.

🐻 Heineken Bear Case

Fair value in this bear case: €77.50 per share

Implied premium to this fair value at a €78.08 share price: about 0.7% above the narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption used in this view: 6.62% a year

  • Builds a valuation mainly from a DCF model using free cash flow projections over 5 years and two different terminal value methods.
  • Arrives at an intrinsic value range of €72 to €91 per share and then applies a 15% margin of safety, which pulls the working fair value down to about €77.50.
  • Highlights that at a share price just above €78, Heineken sits close to this cautious fair value, so any change in cash flow, discount rate or growth assumptions can quickly shift the margin of safety up or down.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It focuses on cash the business can generate for shareholders rather than just current earnings multiples.

For Qiagen, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $466.9 million.   • Analysts' consensus view links this margin picture to future profit quality, as they expect profit margins to move from 8.0% to 9.1% over the next 3 years, which aligns with the current 7.4% margin but also raises the question of whether regions like EMEA and APAC, where revenue fell 27% and 8%, can support that shift without relying on one time items.
    • The consensus narrative highlights higher margin products and more software in the mix as a key driver, which fits with margins already sitting above the 6.7% level from a year ago.
    • At the same time, critics in that narrative point to ClickShare sales falling 16% and ongoing restructuring costs as potential headwinds that could limit how far margins can move up from the current 7.4%.

Earnings Growth Slows Versus Five Year Pace

  • Earnings grew 13.7% over the last year, compared with 34.5% per year over the past five years, so the recent growth rate is well below the longer term average even though trailing EPS is €0.85 on €71.6 million of net income.
  • What stands out in the bullish narrative is the expectation that operational moves like the Wuxi factory, automation investments and a share buyback program of up to €60 million can keep earnings growing, yet the supplied forecasts of about 11% annual earnings growth and 3.8% annual revenue growth are below the Belgian market averages cited and below the historic 34.5% per year earnings pace.
    • The plan to reach earnings of €99.4 million and EPS of €1.11 by around September 2028 builds on the current base of €71.6 million net income and €0.85 EPS, but assumes Barco can avoid a further step down from the recent 13.7% growth rate.
    • Forecasts also assume shares outstanding fall by 2.43% per year over 3 years, so part of the expected EPS progress comes from a smaller share count rather than repeating the earlier 34.5% per year earnings expansion.

Some investors see this mix of slower recent earnings growth, forecast 11% annual gains, and the ongoing share buyback as a key test for the optimistic case on Barco, and they are watching how consistently management turns operational tweaks into EPS progress. 🐂 Barco Bull Case

Value Case Built On 12x P/E And 5.2% Yield

  • At a share price of €10.58 and a trailing EPS of €0.85, Barco trades on a P/E of roughly 12x, compared with peer and industry averages of about 32.9x and 24.9x, plus a 5.2% dividend yield on top.
  • Bears focus on the fact that revenue is only expected to grow around 3.8% per year and earnings around 11% per year, both below the Belgian market figures cited, and argue that even a DCF fair value of about €19.93 and an analyst consensus price target of €14.42 do not fully offset concerns about weaker regions like EMEA and APAC and the 16% decline in ClickShare, especially if one time benefits such as inventory movements and sale and leaseback gains do not repeat.
    • The current gap between the €10.58 share price and the DCF fair value of €19.93, along with the €14.42 analyst target, is sizeable on paper, yet depends on the company reaching forecast revenue of €1.1b and earnings of €99.4 million.
    • Consensus also assumes a future P/E of 14.2x on those 2028 earnings, slightly above the current 12x, which means part of the perceived value case relies on the market being willing to pay a higher multiple than today despite slower revenue growth than the wider Belgian market.
    Next Steps
